posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
I am becoming a St Aubin groupy, drinking plenty of PYCM and Lamy, loving the raciness of the site and the fair cost of entry.
This is another cracker, bright, taut, backward, a stylistic nod to Chablis, showing green apples and citrus, without the sea spray. Racy it is, generous it is not. Just the way I like it aha aha!!

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Sounds great Kent.

A '14 Lamy Remilly was splendid at lunch yesterday. So taut and direct!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Interestingly, Damien Colin (who makes the Marc Colin wines) contends that En Montceau (and not En Remilly or Murgers des Dents de Chien) is the best 1er cru terroir for white in Saint Aubin… and it’s hard to argue with what their 70-year-old vines there produce. Definitely one to add to your shopping list.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: Interesting William, not a Cru you see a lot, but will look out for it.

posts.name: Russ Williams
posts.cooked: Interesting indeed as I don’t think I’ve ever seen it. Anyone know what quantities are produced?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
As I understand it, they own 0.65ha.

If they crop at 45 hl/ha that would be just under 4,000.

(edited to correct surface area)

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: Kent, your note nails it; much the same as my recent experience with this wine. I was not aware of William’s insights here, but I really enjoyed the Montceau, as well, which I recall as having a little more flesh.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Interestingly, Damien Colin (who makes the Marc Colin wines) contends that En Montceau (and not En Remilly or Murgers des Dents de Chien) is the best 1er cru terroir for white in Saint Aubin… and it’s hard to argue with what their 70-year-old vines there produce. Definitely one to add to your shopping list.

Is he the brother of Pierre Yves Colin-Morey?

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Interestingly, Damien Colin (who makes the Marc Colin wines) contends that En Montceau (and not En Remilly or Murgers des Dents de Chien) is the best 1er cru terroir for white in Saint Aubin… and it’s hard to argue with what their 70-year-old vines there produce. Definitely one to add to your shopping list.

When you get within different vineyards, how much of the difference is less which of the vineyards generally is better and in which of the vineyards does the single producer have the better exposure and the older vines?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:

Is he the brother of Pierre Yves Colin-Morey?

There are three: Pierre-Yves, Damien and Joseph. First PY left to set up his own domaine / Maison, and now Joseph has too, so Damien is left in sole charge of Domaine Marc Colin.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:
William Kelley:

When you get within different vineyards, how much of the difference is less which of the vineyards generally is better and in which of the vineyards does the single producer have the better exposure and the older vines?

And their vine genetics & rootstocks, when they harvest, how they vinify. The variables are almost infinite. Clearly, one would rather own an excellent old vine Gevrey villages than a Camus Chambertin, but in this case we’re dealing with finer distinctions.

I’m not sure I have an opinion (yet), but I was interested to hear Damien’s observation—which I think is borne out, at least, by his wines.

En Montceau, in any case, is an interesting spot: the lower portion of Les Champlots, just above the hamlet of Gamay and at the same elevation as the upper portion of En Remilly, turning more towards the east. Not sure where Colin’s vines are within it.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
I have some of his 2014 2014 Puligny "La Garenne"1er Cru, which I have not tried yet. I believe I have heard mixed reports, some complaining about too much or perhaps still unresolved oak? I am no oak-o-phobe (I’ll drink Sauzet with pleasure under the right circumstances) nor an oak-o-phile. Anyone have I chance to try this wine lately; is it in a good place? (I may bring it to a dinner soon, for one thing.)

Thanks in advance!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Joshua Kates:

I have some of his 2014 2014 Puligny "La Garenne"1er Cru, which I have not tried yet. I believe I have heard mixed reports, some complaining about too much or perhaps still unresolved oak? I am no oak-o-phobe (I’ll drink Sauzet with pleasure under the right circumstances) nor an oak-o-phile. Anyone have I chance to try this wine lately; is it in a good place? (I may bring it to a dinner soon, for one thing.)

Thanks in advance!

By 2014, the touch with new oak had become quite light for a while. The really oaky era of Marc Colin was actually when a young Pierre-Yves was making them! He then swung in the opposite direction when he started his own label.

The '14 Puligny Le Trézin was drinking quite well, albeit very youthfully, when I had a glass from a magnum back in September 2017 while passing through the Caveau de Chassagne. If you do open it, decant it.

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked: Thanks for your insights William. I have not had a great deal of experience with the wines of Marc Colin but do recall a Corton Charlemagne from the last noughties that was very good also. Am now certainly keen to procure more and be on the lookout for the En Montceau.

En Montceau, in any case, is an interesting spot: the lower portion of Les Champlots, just above the hamlet of Gamay and at the same elevation as the upper portion of En Remilly, turning more towards the east. Not sure where Colin’s vines are within it.

Thanks. This is why I love Burgundy.
